channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Will Trump Put Boots on the Ground in Iran?
item.description: Thousands more U.S. troops are heading to the Middle East.

item.title: An Iran Exit Plan
item.description: There’s still time for a sensible compromise between Washington and Tehran.

item.title: G-7 Aims to Balance Addressing Russia-Ukraine, Iran Wars
item.description: U.S. allies worry that Washington will deprioritize Kyiv amid the Iran war.

item.title: Republican Support for Trump’s Iran War Is Wobbling
item.description: Lawmakers have concerns about cost, possible ground troops, and unclear objectives.

item.title: How High Could Energy Prices Go?
item.description: Jason Bordoff on the winners and losers from conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: The Asia-Pacific Pivot Is a Zombie Policy
item.description: A much-ballyhooed idea is almost—but not quite—dead.

item.title: Iran Is Putting a ‘Toll Booth’ in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Some ships are getting through: those that pay, and those that play.

item.title: Nepal’s Fresh Start Begins Now
item.description: A new party takes office with a mandate—and high expectations from the Gen Z movement that propelled it to power.

item.title: Understanding the Ongoing France-Vanuatu Negotiations About Matthew and Hunter Islands
item.description: Unpacking the longstanding territorial dispute between Vanuatu and France’s New Caledonia involves untangling complexity, nuance, and sensitivities.

item.title: The Politics of the China-Kyrgyzstan-Uzbekistan Railway
item.description: With the China-Kyrgyzstan-Uzbekistan railway to be completed by 2030, discussion is shifting from regional connectivity to who will benefit most.

item.title: More Questioned, Arrested, in Expanding Kyrgyzneftgas Probe
item.description: This week the former head of the National Bank and the brother of Kamchybek Tashiev were questioned, the former then detained.

item.title: The Mother of Forever Defeats
item.description: A mere three months before launching a war against Iran, a major Middle Eastern power that has no intention of folding, US President Donald Trump had signed a National Security Strategy that expressed a "clear preference for non-intervention in the affairs of other nations.” What happened?

item.title: Hormuz is not the only weak spot for global trade
item.description: Many shipping routes are vulnerable, from the Strait of Malacca to the Panama Canal

item.title: England has shown the world how to replace farm subsidies
item.description: A rare Brexit dividend

item.title: How Chinese companies are reshaping Indonesia
item.description: They are fleeing cut-throat competition at home

item.title: Millions of Burmese struggle to find safety in Thailand
item.description: Many refugees are left exposed

item.title: China is breaking into one of the world’s weirdest car markets
item.description: An island of American motoring in Central Asia is changing fast

item.title: China’s huge pork industry is a victim of its own success
item.description: The country is awash in its favourite meat

item.title: Does the Iran war increase the risk of a Chinese attack on Taiwan?
item.description: China has reason to wait, but its window of opportunity has widened

item.title: Jiang Shengnan is the most vocal woman in Chinese politics
item.description: She is trying to push change from within the system

item.title: For Donald Trump, Cuba is everything Iran is not
item.description: A breakthrough is in his grasp. But his idea of progress may be a throwback to another era

item.title: Snarled airports and frozen funding test the new DHS secretary
item.description: Markwayne Mullin promises a kinder, gentler department

item.title: America’s spies have a lot to complain about
item.description: They are grappling with politicisation, purges and a president who may not want to hear them

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Russia’s latest attack on free speech
item.description: The government wants to limit contact with the outside world, as well as private communication at home

item.title: Germany’s Social Democrats gaze into the abyss
item.description: But an electoral drubbing could open a window for national reforms

item.title: France offers some hope for defeating populists
item.description: The mainstream parties get the biggest prizes

item.title: The war’s biggest corporate winners and losers may surprise you
item.description: Markets are beginning to signal the long-term consequences of the surge in fuel prices

item.title: Will the EU’s new merger rules unleash a wave of dealmaking?
item.description: Don’t hold your breath

item.title: A new case of chip smuggling shows the limits of export controls
item.description: Contraband AI gear continues to make its way to China

item.title: Welcome to emoji school
item.description: OK boomers! Your questions answered

item.title: Iran’s regime walls off the internet
item.description: The latest shutdown looks designed to be permanent

item.title: Israeli settlers are growing more violent in the West Bank
item.description: Since the war with Iran began, attacks against Palestinians have become more systematic

item.title: In the current Gulf war, water may prove as decisive as oil
item.description: The Arab states are plainly vulnerable, but so is Iran

item.title: Brazil has a secret weapon against oil shocks
item.description: Biofuels will help the country fend off the effects of conflict in the Middle East

item.title: Mexico’s broken economy
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum’s government is tinkering, not reforming

item.title: Russia wants to limit contact with the outside world
item.description: A recent internet blackout reflects the Kremlin’s nervousness

item.title: Politics
The Security Architecture of the Taiwan-US Trade Deal
item.description: Read carefully, the Agreement on Reciprocal Trade is something far more consequential than a mere trade deal.

item.title: Japan to Send Combat Units to Philippines-US Balikatan Exercises for the First Time
item.description: The move represents a historic shift in Japan’s security engagement with Southeast Asia – and Manila specifically.

item.title: What the Engagement Debate Misses: Visiting China Is Not the Same as Understanding It
item.description: When U.S. policymakers visit China, they encounter a curated performance, not a country. A different approach to engagement is possible – but it requires abandoning the comfort of the guided tour.

item.title: Pakistan’s Attacks and the World’s Silence Over Afghanistan
item.description: The illegitimacy of the Taliban does not mean that the fundamental rights of the Afghan people have been suspended.

item.title: Trump’s Tariff War Has Failed on Every Front
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s tariffs and the uncertainty surrounding them have sent shockwaves through the global economy but have failed to achieve any of their stated objectives. Restoring an open, multilateral trading system will not be easy, but eliminating his arbitrary measures will be a necessary first step.

item.title: Libya, Iran, and the Limits of Airpower
item.description: What a previous intervention reveals about the hard choices that U.S. President Donald Trump faces.

item.title: How Pakistan is Redefining Middle Power Agency in the US-Israel War on Iran
item.description: Islamabad’s emergence as a channel illustrates how middle powers can punch above their weight when larger actors are hemmed in by their own alignments.

item.title: Japan’s Response to the De Facto Blockade of the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The key question: Is it a ‘survival-threating situation’?

item.title: How Much AI-Driven Productivity Growth Do We Want?
item.description: As AI advances, societies must consider how to strike a balance between the disruption caused by rapid productivity growth and its many benefits, including rising incomes and living standards. Fortunately, most advanced economies are well equipped to absorb the shocks associated with breakthrough technologies.

item.title: China is winning the AI talent race
item.description: Its lead over the West is only set to widen

item.title: How long will Israel stay in Lebanon?
item.description: The threat of another occupation is tearing at the fabric of Lebanese society

item.title: Why the US Won’t Be Making a Pivot to Asia Anytime Soon
item.description: Stated intentions are one thing. Reality is quite another.

item.title: The end of the world’s longest-running Maoist insurgency
item.description: A report from one of the Naxalites’ final redoubts

item.title: Donald Trump says he is close to a deal with Iran
item.description: Almost no one believes that talks are getting anywhere

item.title: Advantage Iran
item.description: A month of bombing has achieved nothing. Will Donald Trump escalate, or talk?

item.title: The decline and fall of the Roman currency empire
item.description: The fate of ancient coins offers uncomfortable lessons about dollar dominance

item.title: Europe should think twice before weakening its merger rules
item.description: A strict competition policy is not the barrier to bigger firms

item.title: The case against energy bail-outs
item.description: As war rages in Iran, governments must not repeat the mistakes of 2022

item.title: Christine Lagarde’s sober tone on the Gulf war energy shock
item.description: Our interview with the ECB’s president

item.title: The human toll of the Iran war, in charts and maps
item.description: Our analysis suggests that more than 22m people across the Middle East live near a reported strike

item.title: America’s pro-Israel lobby is facing a backlash
item.description: As attitudes to Israel shift, AIPAC is shifting its tactics

item.title: What’s Next for Cuba?
item.description: From Venezuela to Iran, US President Donald Trump’s administration has apparently embraced a simple mantra: attack now, plan later. In Cuba’s case, the attack has so far taken the form not of airstrikes, but of blockade on fuel imports, which has produced a severe economic and humanitarian crisis. But while Trump speaks glibly of the Cuban regime’s “imminent demise” and a “friendly takeover” of the country, he has offered no vision for the island’s future, though he may have created a blueprint for China to apply in Taiwan.

item.title: The Big Picture
Indian Government Cracks Down on Video Reels Lampooning PM Modi
item.description: Authorities took down a video that mocked Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s undignified behavior while meeting foreign leaders.

item.title: Where Have All the Allies Gone?
item.description: The multilateral system that US President Donald Trump has dismantled was not, as he believes, a trap laid by weaker states to fleece a credulous superpower. It was infrastructure that took decades to build – and whose wreckage lies in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Iran’s Civilizational Rhetoric Is Hollow
item.description: The Islamic Republic claims to be thinking in terms of centuries, but its actions suggest otherwise.

item.title: The United States Has Become a Rogue State
item.description: Here’s what the rest of the world can do about it.

item.title: How to Get to Free and Fair Elections in Venezuela
item.description: Which of the country’s dueling leaders is in favor with Trump is beside the point.

item.title: The Age of Asymmetric Warfare Is Here, and the West Is Not Ready
item.description: Ukraine has much to teach the United States about drone defense.

item.title: The False Promise of “Flexible Realism”
item.description: Trump’s war on Iran reveals a foreign policy without principles.

item.title: Iran’s Long Game
item.description: Decades of preparation are paying off.

item.title: The Islamic State Is Targeting Youth in Malaysia
Thai Tanker Transits Strait of Hormuz After Agreement Between Bangkok and Tehran
item.description: The passage comes two weeks after a Thailand-flagged bulk carrier was hit and damaged by Iranian projectiles while transiting the Strait.

item.title: Is the Philippines Really About to Restart Joint Oil Exploration With China in the South China Sea?
item.description: Even in the context of the current "energy emergency," President Marcos' proposal would have to surmount considerable constitutional and political hurdles.

item.title: Meta and Google face a reckoning over social-media addiction
item.description: A landmark verdict in California could have far-reaching consequences

item.title: NASA’s Moon-base plans mark a rethinking of its future
item.description: The changes are welcome

item.title: How the Iran War Will Reconfigure Militancy in Balochistan
item.description: Security forces in Pakistan are “preparing for a further push from terrorist outfits in Balochistan" due to the ongoing conflict.

item.title: Configuring India’s Security for a Multi-front War
item.description: India’s defense budget has fallen steadily from 4 percent of its GDP in the late 1980s to under 2 percent today.
